heading: 2.1 Commonly Used Visualisations for VBD Data
text: Different types data need different types visualisations.Choosing appropriate plot best represent data important communicate data proposed patterns clearly accurately.VBD research, common visualisations might come across include:Scatter plots - useful explore relationships variables.Boxplots - useful compare distributions groups.Line plots - useful visualise trends time.Bar plots - useful compare values groups.Maps - useful show spatial patterns.vector surveillance research, one useful frequently used visualisations abundance plots. can used show vector counts change time across locations.Abundance plots typically use lines communicate overall trends vector populations. However, points can also used show individual observations within dataset. can particularly useful exploratory analysis allows us visualise variation data identify outliers.Throughout training, focus developing effective abundance plots real VBD datasets. start simple exploratory abundance plots Pre- Live Session content, build complex abundance plots Live Session.

heading: 2.2 What can Visualisations Tell Us About Data?
text: Effective visualisations can help us communicate complex datasets quickly identifying distributions patterns data can unclear dataframes alone.Visualising data can help us identify:Distribution data - values spread across dataset, including spatial distribution vectors pathogens.Correlative relationships - potential associations multiple variables, vector populations environmental factors.Temporal trends - variable changes time, example, vector abundance time.Inter-group comparisons - differences groups, instance, vector species across regions.Outliers anomalies - unexpected observations may suggest errors addressed modelling.Given much information can extract , visualisations often first step exploratory data analysis statistical modelling.

heading: 2.2.1 Task 1: Visualising Tick Abundance Across Locations
text:
Let’s try visualising VBD data. First, download tick_dataset_wrangled.csv open RStudio:dataset contains tick abundance, sample_value, across two sampling locations, sample_location.people make mistakes: Remember save tick_dataset_wrangled.csv appropriate working directory set working directory correctly RStudio!can plot data visualise difference abundance across two sample locations. might typically see bar plots used visualise abundance across two groups, use scatter plot show individual observations, allows us assess variation identify potential outliers dataset., use ggplot2 package. ggplot2 one commonly used packages visualisations graphics, useful understand code package. particularly good build plots step--step defining:dataset want use.variables want visualise.type plot want generate.Run code generate simple abundance plot tick_data using points show individual observations:now see simple abundance plot “Plot” window RStudio, looks like :Tip: Using ggplot2 code visualisations can look heavy, breaking step plot like building blocks. Let’s closer look:ggplot(tick_data, aes(x = sample_location, y = sample_value))
tick_data dataset want visualise.
aes() represents aesthetics, including variables dataset want visualise. plot:
x = sample_location tells R place sampling location x-axis.
y = sample_value tells R place tick abundance y-axis.geom_point()
Tells R plot point observation dataset. data, one observation tick count per sample collected particular location. change wanted use line plot.labs() function simply adds labels visualisation make easier interpret. plot, added x-axis label, y-axis label, title plot.good idea save visualisations can easily refer back need. several ways save visualisations R, good practice use code:ggsave("tick_abundance_location.png", plot = tick_abundance_location)Now visualised data, identify patterns extract information dataset.visualisation, can see:Tick abundance appears greater Sogn Fjordane Akershus Østfold.observations across locations show relatively low tick abundance, small number samples showing much higher values.Sogn Fjordane samples Akershus Østfold, suggesting potential bias sampling effort. something may need addressed running analysis.Although Sogn Fjordane samples include high tick counts, values spread across several observations, suggesting genuine variation data rather single outliers.
code:
tick_data <- read.csv("tick_dataset_wrangled.csv")
tick_data
library("ggplot2")

tick_abundance_location <- ggplot(tick_data, aes(x = sample_location, y = sample_value)) +
geom_point() +
labs(
x = "Sampling Location",
y = "Tick Abundance",
title = "Tick Abundance Across Sampling Locations"
)

tick_abundance_location
tick_abundance_location
labs(
x = "Sampling Location",
y = "Tick Abundance",
title = "Tick Abundance Across Sampling Locations"
)

heading: 2.3 Formulating Hypotheses From Visualisations
text:
Now know patterns can drawn data visualisations, can begin develop hypotheses mechanisms processes might explain patterns.hypothesis testable explanation observed pattern.example, visualised dataset Culicoides abundance time observed pattern showing higher Culicoides counts summer months, might suggest following hypothesis:
“Higher temperatures summer provide optimal conditions Culicoides larval development, leading increased abundance season.”Similarly, plotted dataset sandfly abundance across different habitat types observed pattern indicating higher sandfly counts peri-domestic habitats, might suggest hypothesis:
“Peri-domestic environments increase sandfly abundance providing suitable breeding habitats, organic waste cattle sheds.”examples, can understand visualisations can help generate data-driven research questions hypotheses.Important: Remember, data visualisations alone show causation. can used tool highlight potential patterns tested using statistical analysis.

heading: 3.4 Building on Abundance Plots
text: Pre- Live Session content, covered simple abundance plots considered visualisations can used help identify potential patterns exploratory analysis vector surveillance data.However, simple abundance plots often tell us part story. VBD research, typically want understand abundance patterns vary across time, space, species.Common VBD research questions consider:vector abundance change throughout year?certain locations consistently report higher vector counts?different species show distinct seasonal patterns?session, build basic abundance plots introduced Pre- Live Session content developing complex visualisations using R ggplot2 package.

heading: 3.4.1 Abundance Across Sampling Locations
text: Vector populations often vary locations. Differences habitat, climate, host availability, land use can influence vector abundance. result, combining data multiple sampling sites single trend may obscure important spatial patterns.Pre-Live Session content, plotted abundance ticks across two sampling locations. , used scatter plot view individual observations practice identifying patterns data. However, VBD research, typically use bar plot visualise abundance across multiple locations.Let’s start downloading mosquito_subset_wrangled.csv. Open dataset RStudio load required packages:Note: tidyverse -encompassing package contains bunch extra packages.mostly using functions ggplot2 lubridate packages, can just load using tidyverse.data involves multiple samples site, next want summarise abundance data location. grouping location using group_by() function, summarising abundance per location mean() sd() using summarise() function:can use ggplot2 visualise abundance mosquitos multiple sampling locations using similar code Pre- Live Session content. start stating dataset variables want x- y-axes:time, instead using geom_point(), use geom_col() geom_errorbar(). tells R want plot bars whiskers (vertical error lines):code starting look heavy, just building aesthetics.geom_col(), can use fill set colour bars.geom_errorbar(), can use aes() set aesthetics. case:use ymin set lower end whisker, calculated mean minus standard deviation abundance.use ymax set upper end whisker, calculated mean plus standard deviation abundance.width controls wide horizontal lines either end whisker.final chunk code adding labels using labs() - practised Pre- Live Session content:piece chunks code together, can generate visualisation:Tip: Remember save graphic:can now use visualisation compare abundance patterns across sampling sites:Mean abundance varies across locations, Sorragna Terre Del Reno showing highest mean mosquito abundance, Gattatico showing lowest mean mosquito abundance.lot variation within location. consistent across dataset, suggesting natural variation data explored .Visualisations can help researchers identify potential hotspots vector activity guide investigation ecological factors driving patterns. graphic, can see lot variation within location. One way assess detail consider abundance time.
code:
library("tidyverse")

mosquito_data <- read_csv("data/mosquito_subset_wrangled.csv")
#> Rows: 1991 Columns: 14
#> ── Column specification ────────────────────────────────────
#> Delimiter: ","
#> chr  (9): genus, species, sample_unit, sample_sex, sampl...
#> dbl  (3): sample_value, sample_lat_dd, sample_long_dd
#> date (2): sample_start_date, sample_end_date
#> 
#> ℹ Use `spec()` to retrieve the full column specification for this data.
#> ℹ Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message.
abundance_per_location <- mosquito_data %>%
  group_by(sample_location) %>%
  summarise(
    mean_abundance = mean(sample_value, na.rm = TRUE),
    se_abundance = sd(sample_value, na.rm = TRUE) / sqrt(sum(!is.na(sample_value)))
  )ggplot(
  abundance_per_location,
  aes(x = sample_location, y = mean_abundance)
  ) +geom_col(fill = "darkturquoise") +
  geom_errorbar(aes(
    ymin = mean_abundance - se_abundance,
    ymax = mean_abundance + se_abundance
  ),
  width = 0.2) +
labs(
  title = "Mean Abundance of Culex pipiens Across Locations",
  x = "Sampling Location",
  y = "Mean Abundance"
)
abundance_plot_across_locations <- ggplot(
  abundance_per_location,
  aes(x = sample_location, y = mean_abundance)
  ) +
  geom_col(fill = "darkturquoise") +
  geom_errorbar(
    aes(
      ymin = mean_abundance - se_abundance,
      ymax = mean_abundance + se_abundance
    ),
    width = 0.2) +
  labs(
    title = "Mean Abundance of Culex pipiens Across Locations",
    x = "Sampling Location",
    y = "Mean Abundance"
  )

abundance_plot_across_locations
ggsave("daily_abundance_all.pdf", plot = daily_abundance_plot_all)

heading: 3.4.2 Abundance Over Time: Time-Series
text: time-series plot shows observations across continuous timeline, allowing us see abundance changes time. visualisations can help us identify:Seasonal trends abundance.Periods rapid population growth decline.Potential sampling gaps inconsistencies.Understanding patterns particularly important VBD research vector populations often strongly influenced seasonal environmental conditions, including temperature, rainfall, host availability.Pre- Live Session content, grouped observations months. now going visualise abundance true time-series using sampling date show abundance changes continuous time single species.Later session, consider abundance multiple locations, now, let’s filter data visualise abundance time single location - Goro:working time-series data, useful understand R stores processes dates. dataset, dates typically stored characters (plain text format). Although entry like “2023-06-15” might look like date us, R automatically recognise date treat character string.generate plot, ggplot2 respond variable stored. example, variables character format plotted categorical, whereas variables date format plotted along continuous time axis.time-series visualisations, continuous time axis ensures:Observations plotted chronological order.Spaces data points reflect true time differences.Trends time can interpreted accurately.convert date column characters date objects, can use .Date() function:.Date() function used convert column proper date format R ggplot2 can recognise dates.Frequent mistake: dates messy, plot look messy. Ensuring date column correct data format means R knows process data correctly.can build visualisation ggplot2, Pre- Live Session content. start stating dataset variables want x- y-axes:Pre- Live Session content, used geom_point() generate simple abundance plot scatter points see individual observations across specific months (categories). true time-series, want visualise time continuous measure across observation dates, appropriate use line plot. , replace geom_point() geom_line():can start consider visual appeal graphics, starting colour line. Within geom_line() function, can set colour line:Setting line colours can useful plotting multiple lines graphic. develop idea later session.Tip: Although visually pleasing graphics nice look , often important understand develop effective visualisations accurately communicate data patterns.cover skills workshop, general, try consider plain clear interpretable graphic often useful flashy graphic poor accuracy readability.can add title axes labels using labs() Pre- Live Session content, generate abundance plot:Oh dear, graphic looks rather messy! Although used correct code ensured date column correct format, visualisation still difficult read interpret. real-world VBD data often includes multiple samples collected data. create effective time-series visualisation, need summarise samples single value per time point:can now use summarised daily abundance data try plotting , time using daily_abundance_goro data total_abundance y-axis:much better! visualisation effectively shows trend mosquito abundance time selected location.plot:x-axis represents continuous sampling time period.y-axis represents mosquito abundance.line connects daily abundance continuous time.Time-series abundance plots like one can provide useful insights seasonal dynamics within vector populations. can useful identifying patterns data:Strong seasonality - abundance spikes summer year.Year--year variability - years higher peaks others.General trend - although abundance varies within year, peak year seems increasing time.Tip: Remember save graphic:
code:
mosquito_data_goro <- mosquito_data %>%
  filter(sample_location == "Goro")
mosquito_data_goro$sample_start_date <- as.Date(mosquito_data_goro$sample_start_date)ggplot(mosquito_data_goro, aes(x = sample_start_date, y = sample_value)) +ggplot(mosquito_data_goro, aes(x = sample_start_date, y = sample_value)) +
  geom_line() +ggplot(mosquito_data_goro, aes(x = sample_start_date, y = sample_value)) +
  geom_line(colour = "darkturquoise") +
time_series_plot_goro <- ggplot(mosquito_data_goro, aes(x = sample_start_date, y = sample_value)) +
  geom_line(colour = "darkturquoise") +
  labs(
    title = "Abundance of Culex pipiens Over Time",
    x = "Date",
    y = "Abundance"
  )

time_series_plot_goro
daily_abundance_goro <- mosquito_data_goro %>%
  group_by(sample_start_date) %>%
  summarise(total_abundance = sum(sample_value, na.rm = TRUE))
daily_abundance_plot_goro <- ggplot(daily_abundance_goro, aes(x = sample_start_date, y = total_abundance)) +
  geom_line(colour = "darkturquoise") +
  labs(
    title = "Abundance of Culex pipiens Over Time",
    x = "Date",
    y = "Abundance"
  )

daily_abundance_plot_goro
ggsave("daily_abundance_all.pdf", plot = daily_abundance_plot_all)

heading: 3.4.3 Abundance Across Multiple Locations Over Time
text: previous examples, visualised abundance multiple sampling locations, abundance changes time single location, combine look abundance across multiple locations time?VBD research, data commonly collected multiple sampling sites, often want compare vector abundance varies across locations simultaneously.can achieve developing time series plot include multiple locations. , grouped data date alone, time, want group data sampling location date, abundance calculated separately location time:Frequent mistake: sure add .groups = "keep" adding multiple variables group_by. Without , packages R drop variables first group_by variable, see message like :summarise() grouped output 'sample_location'. can override using .groups argument.visualise data, generate time-series abundance plot , time, use group = sample_location argument within aes() tell R want separate line different sampling location:visualisation wanted, looks bit messy. lines displayed colour, makes plot difficult read. can see patterns abundance change time, difficult distinguish different locations.seeing complex visualisations always better visualisations, especially interpretation limited.One option improve visualisation help distinguish locations assign different colour sampling location. , map sampling_location colour aesthetic within aes(), rather assigning line colour within geom_line():adding colour, line now represents different sampling location clearly, key detailing line colour location. makes easier identify patterns across locations.plotting location time together, can identify patterns data:Strong seasonal patterns across locations, patterns sharp peaks rapid declines year.Although trends time similar across locations, show higher abundance others.extreme peaks abundance stand compared general trends. may represent natural variation, optimal conditions location mosquito breeding, observations outliers. testing needed assess .Tip: Remember save graphic:However, using colour can definitely improve graphics, visualisations can still feel overcrowded many groups displayed together. example, visualisation plots 8 different locations graph. Overlapping lines similar temporal patterns can make difficult fully interpret data.Additionally, using colour alone can cause difficulties accessibility audiences, address later session.
code:
daily_abundance_all_locations <- mosquito_data %>%
  group_by(sample_location, sample_start_date) %>%
  summarise(total_abundance = sum(sample_value, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "keep")
daily_abundance_plot_all <- ggplot(
  daily_abundance_all_locations,
  aes(x = sample_start_date, y = total_abundance, group = sample_location)
) +
  geom_line(colour = "darkturquoise") +
  labs(
    title = "Abundance of Culex pipiens Across Locations Over Time",
    x = "Date",
    y = "Abundance"
  )

daily_abundance_plot_all
daily_abundance_plot_all <- ggplot(
  daily_abundance_all_locations,
  aes(x = sample_start_date, y = total_abundance, colour = sample_location)
) +
  geom_line() +
  labs(
    title = "Abundance of Culex pipiens Across Locations Over Time",
    x = "Date",
    y = "Abundance",
    colour = "Sampling Location"
  )

daily_abundance_plot_all
ggsave("daily_abundance_all.pdf", plot = daily_abundance_plot_all)

heading: 3.4.4 Using Faceting to Improve Clarity
text: One useful solution overcrowding faceting, feature ggplot2 splits single plot multiple smaller panels.panel displays subset data, allowing patterns compared side--side without overlapping elements. separating data smaller panels, can often reveal patterns might otherwise hidden within crowded visualisation.Faceting particularly useful :Comparing multiple species.Comparing sampling sites.Exploring patterns across environmental conditions.can apply faceting previous time-series visualisation separate sampling location panel, rather using different colours. directly addresses overlapping lines, improving overall clarity readability., add facet_wrap() existing code, tells R want generate several panels separated location:separating sampling location panel, patterns data become much clearer:Seasonal trends still evident across locations, now able see patterns vary locations easily.Differences abundance magnitude much clearer, locations consistently showing higher peaks, others reflecting relatively low abundance across time-series.Extreme peaks can clearly associated specific locations without overlapping lines hiding observations.Overall, faceting improves clarity reducing visual clutter allowing direct comparisons locations. makes easier interpret independent trends per location compare patterns across multiple locations.Optional refinement: current visualisation, panels share y-axis scale, good comparisons. However, scale also means extreme peaks visually dominant, locations smaller peaks look flat comparison.can add scales = "free_y" within facet_wrap() adjust y-axis scales across panels, allowing us visualise patterns per location greater detail:
code:
daily_abundance_plot_faceted <- ggplot(
  daily_abundance_all_locations,
  aes(x = sample_start_date, y = total_abundance)
) +
  geom_line(colour = "darkturquoise") +
  facet_wrap(~ sample_location) +
  labs(
    title = "Abundance of Culex pipiens Across Locations Over Time",
    x = "Date",
    y = "Abundance"
  )

daily_abundance_plot_faceted
daily_abundance_plot_faceted_Y <- ggplot(
  daily_abundance_all_locations,
  aes(x = sample_start_date, y = total_abundance)
) +
  geom_line(colour = "darkturquoise") +
  facet_wrap(~ sample_location, scales = "free_y") +
  labs(
    title = "Abundance of Culex pipiens Across Locations Over Time",
    x = "Date",
    y = "Abundance"
  )

daily_abundance_plot_faceted_Y

heading: 3.4.5 Applying These Principles to Multiple Species and Datasets
text: Various vector species can exhibit different ecological behaviours. instance:species may emerge earlier season.may reach higher peak abundance.Others may vary abundance depending particular habitats hosts.Understanding differences important studying disease transmission dynamics. Luckily, already tools visualise patterns!last example, visualised abundance time, grouped sampling location. multiple species comparisons, abundance plots grouped species, rather sampling location.important advantage using flexible tools R ggplot2. understand principles building effective visualisations, can apply many different datasets research questions.

heading: 3.5 Drawing Hypotheses from Complex Visualisations
text:
Pre- Live Session content, looked simple visualisations can help us identify patterns data develop initial hypotheses.Real-world VBD datasets often complex, containing observations collected across multiple locations, species, time periods. visualised, datasets can reveal nuanced patterns, allowing researchers develop detailed targeted hypotheses.Let’s go building earlier examples consider complex visualisations can help us refine scientific questions. Based visualisation, hypotheses generate mosquito abundance?Tip: Consider prompts formulate hypotheses:Patterns timeDifferences locations
Unusual observationsAlternative explanations patternWhile visualisations extremely useful identifying patterns, important remember confirm causal relationships. Instead, provide starting point developing hypotheses can later tested using statistical models, experimental studies, additional data collection.practice, process often involves several steps:Identifying patterns visualisation.Proposing possible explanations patterns.Considering alternative explanations.Designing analyses studies test hypotheses.working process, researchers can move simple visual observations well-defined scientific questions contribute deeper understanding vector ecology disease transmission dynamics.

heading: 3.6 What Makes a Good Visualisation?
text: far workshop, focused create visualisations explore patterns vector surveillance data. However, seen, visualisations communicate information clearly effectively.well-designed visualisation help viewer quickly understand key message data. Poorly designed visualisations, hand, can confusing, misleading, difficult interpret.now explore characteristics contribute effective data visualisations, based experience visualisations previously seen. Consider think makes good bad visualisation.features commonly associated good visualisations include:Clarity - plot communicates message quickly clearly.Accuracy - visual representation reflects underlying data correctly.Simplicity - unnecessary elements distract data avoided.Accessibility - visualisation can interpreted wide audience.example, clear axis labels, readable text, appropriate colours contribute making visualisation easier interpret.Conversely, poor visualisations may include limitations :Unclear missing axis labels.Confusing colour schemes.Unnecessary visual elements.Misleading scales distorted representation data.issues can make difficult viewers understand patterns communicated visualisation.

heading: 3.7 Collaborative Task: Improving a Flawed Visualisation
text: explore concepts practice, now work collaboratively improve series intentionally flawed visualisations.split breakout rooms, group given one visualisation contains different common visualisation mistake. may include:Confusing colour schemes.Unclear axis labels.Inappropriate chart types.Missing legends.Excessive visual clutter.goal activity identify problems within visualisation work together redesign underlying dataset associated patterns communicated clearly.need work together :Identify makes visualisation difficult interpret.Modify code create improved version visualisation.prepared briefly explain changes group made.Note single “correct” solution. goal task think critically visualisation design choices influence data interpreted.Tip: Consider questions :main message visualisation?aspects current design make message difficult interpret?visualisation improved?activity contributes experience collaborative critique, useful skill research settings. Real-world visualisations often refined feedback colleagues collaborators.

heading: 3.7.1 Graph 1
text: Dataset: group1_and_2_data.csvFlawed code:
code:
library("tidyverse")

group1_data <- read.csv("data/group1_and_2_data.csv")

group1_data$sample_start_date <- as.Date(group1_data$sample_start_date)

daily_abundance_all <- group1_data %>%
  group_by(sample_location, sample_start_date) %>%
  summarise(total_abundance = sum(sample_value, na.rm = TRUE),
            .groups = "keep")

group1_plot <- ggplot(daily_abundance_all,
       aes(x = sample_start_date, y = total_abundance, group = sample_location)) +
  geom_line(colour = "darkturquoise") +
  labs(
    title = "Mosquito Abundance Over Time",
    x = "Date",
    y = "Abundance"
  )

group1_plot

heading: 3.7.2 Graph 2
text: Dataset: group1_and_2_data.csvFlawed code:
code:
library("tidyverse")

group2_data <- read.csv("data/group1_and_2_data.csv")

group2_data_cadeo <- group2_data %>%
  filter(sample_location == "Cadeo")

group2_data_cadeo$sample_start_date <- as.Date(group2_data_cadeo$sample_start_date)

group2_plot <- ggplot(group2_data_cadeo,
       aes(x = sample_start_date, y = sample_value)) +
  geom_col(fill = "darkturquoise") +
  labs(
    title = "Mosquito Abundance Over Time",
    x = "Date",

heading: 3.7.3 Graph 3
text: Dataset: group3_and_4_data.csvFlawed code:
code:
library("tidyverse")

group3_data <- read.csv("data/group3_and_4_data.csv")

group3_data$sample_start_date <- as.Date(group3_data$sample_start_date)

daily_abundance <- group3_data %>%
  group_by(sample_location, sample_start_date) %>%
  summarise(total_abundance = sum(sample_value, na.rm = TRUE),
            .groups = "keep")

group3_plot <- ggplot(daily_abundance,
       aes(x = sample_start_date, y = total_abundance, colour = sample_location)) +
  geom_line()

group3_plot

heading: 3.7.4 Graph 4
text: Dataset: group3_and_4_data.csvFlawed code:
code:
library("tidyverse")

group4_data <- read.csv("data/group3_and_4_data.csv")

group4_data$sample_start_date <- as.Date(group4_data$sample_start_date)

daily_abundance_all <- group4_data %>%
  group_by(sample_start_date) %>%
  summarise(total_abundance = sum(sample_value, na.rm = TRUE))

group4_plot <- ggplot(daily_abundance_all,
       aes(x = sample_start_date, y = total_abundance)) +
  geom_line(colour = "darkturquoise") +
  labs(
    title = "Mosquito Abundance Over Time",

heading: 3.9.3 Designing Accessible Visualisations
text: Accessibility important consideration designing visualisations, particularly communicating data diverse audiences. instance, viewers may visual impairments, colour vision deficiencies, can make certain visualisations difficult interpret.multiple principles can help improve accessibility visualisations. commonly used strategies consider include:1. Using accessible colour palettes - colour combinations can difficult differentiate, red green. ggplot2 includes viridis colour scales, designed improve visual clarity accessibility visualisations: scale_colour_viridis_d()2. Using multiple visual cues - using alternative visual cues ensures elements visualisations remain clear visible without colour. Alternative options include line types, point shapes, faceting.3. Exporting visualisations - may noticed throughout workshop, saved visualisations pdf files. format improves accessibility allowing viewers view details clearly zooming without reducing image quality.4. Providing alternative text (alt-text) - sharing visualisations digital formats, alternative text descriptions can added screen reader users can access visualisations.Designing accessible visualisations improves inclusivity results clearer effective communication viewers.

Linear regression models commonly used type linear model, particularly useful response predictor variables continuous. use linear regression models quantify relationship two variables can identify trends make predictions data.Linear regression models fit straight line relationship response variable continuous explanatory variable, can shown equation:

y = mx + c
\]code linear regression model, can use following syntax:Tip: naming models objects R, try use specific names know object explicitly. Using short names like model1, model2, etc. can get confusing.Frequent Mistake:R handle spaces object names. options alternative syntax, recommend using camelCase letters capitalised indicate new words (e.g. specificModelName), using underscore connect words (e.g. specific_model_name).using camelCase, remember R case sensitive - object named specificModelName, call specificmodelname, R show error:view output linear regression model, can use summary() function:show lot information (useful familiarise ), workshop, primarily interested Coefficients table.table one row coefficient model - two rows simple linear regression model: Intercept Slope.Intercept estimate represents predicted value response variable explanatory variable zero.Slope estimate proportional correlation coefficient response variable explanatory variable, scaled ratio standard deviations. Put simply, estimate represents strength (value) direction (positive negative) relationship response explanatory variables.Coefficient table also shows details t-test, tells us whether slope intercept significantly different zero.Std. Error - far observations sit regression line.t value - estimate divided standard error.Pr(>|t|) - p-value, used inform probability observing result true coefficient zero.Tip:

heading: A.1.2 ANOVA Models
text: happens explanatory variable categorical, rather continuous? ANOVA models come , ANalysis VAriance across means different groups categories. ANOVA useful type linear model, particularly response variable continuous, explanatory variable categorical.Coding ANOVA essentially linear regression model - still use lm() function fit relationship response explanatory variable. However, now explanatory variable categorical, slope represents difference group (category) means., can use summary() function check model output view Coefficients table (laid way linear regression model output).Despite looking , differences ANOVA output:Intercept mean first category.Slope difference mean first category (named) category.summary() function can used assess differences specific categories, anova() function can tell us whether categorical explanatory variable whole significant effect response variable.output anova() function also provides table, lists following statistics:Df - refers number categories observations.Sum Sq & Mean Sq - measures variance within categories.F-value - ratio explained variance unexplained variance (higher F-value indicates categorical variable significant effect).Pr(>F) - p-value, used inform probability observing F-value true difference categories.Tip:Use summary() interested category means differences explanatory variable.Use anova() interested overall significance explanatory variable response variable.interested linear models, can find resources :Linear Models: Multiple explanatory variablesLinear Models: Multiple variables interactionsGeneralised Linear ModelsThis additional content optional, necessary complete workshop.

anova(specificModelName)

chapter: A Recap of Linear Models & Basic Plots (optional)
heading: A.2.3 Boxplots
text: Boxplots used compare distribution continuous variable across different groups categories. particularly useful response variable continuous, explanatory variable categorical.boxplot summarises distribution data using five key statistics:minimum valueThe lower quartile (Q1)medianThe upper quartile (Q3)maximum valueThe box represents interquartile range (IQR), contains middle 50% data. Values fall outside range may appear outliers.Boxplots summarise distribution variation data, show individual observations. Therefore, detailed variation within dataset may visible.plot simple boxplot, can use boxplot() function:boxplot(myDataframe$continuousVariable ~ myDataframe$categoricalVariable)
